page_content:
# Passage 4:  
Plasma, which is a mixture of ions, electrons, and neutral atoms and molecules, is of increasing importance in modern medicine. “Cold plasmas” (plasmas with temperatures around 35°C) can be used to sterilize tissue and aid in wound treatment.  
Figure 1 shows the design of the overall components of a cold plasma “pencil.” The plasma plume can be applied to any tissue (such as a wound) in order to kill pathogens.  
Figure 1Schematic of a plasma pencil
The circular electrodes are connected to the voltage source so that they have equal magnitude, opposite sign electric potentials. The voltage between the electrodes varies in time (Figure 2) and creates a spatially uniform electric field between the two electrodes. The electric field provides a medium for periodic electric discharges between the electrodes.  
Figure 2Time variation of the absolute value of the voltage between electrodes
These discharges ionize the gas trapped between the electrodes, and ionized particles with high energy can escape as a plasma plume through an opening in the right electrode. The gas composition can be controlled to produce the plasma most reactive and damaging to a given pathogen.  
Depending on the parameters of the plasma used, sterilization occurs by contact with specific ions generated in the plasma (such as OH−, O−, and O3−) that disrupt the pathogen’s biological functions. Electric charge buildup on some pathogens causes the cell wall to rupture. Sterilization is also caused by the UV radiation (200–300 nm) generated by the electron transitions within the plasma plume.
